sections.title: Discharge Status
sections.content: The patient has completed the residential treatment center (RTC) program and is approaching discharge. The patient feels ready to transition to a Partial Hospitalization Program (PHP). At the time of discharge, the patient is not considered an imminent danger to self or others. Sleep and appetite are reported as good, with energy and concentration back to baseline [T264058].

sections.title: Location
sections.content: The patient was admitted to the Adult Residential Treatment Center (RTC) program for treatment of psychiatric conditions including Major Depressive Disorder, Generalized Anxiety Disorder, Cannabis Use Disorder (severe), and Posttraumatic Stress Disorder (chronic). The patient participated in individual and group therapy sessions, medication management, and other supportive psychotherapy interventions during their stay. Upon discharge from RTC on 03/11/2025, the patient planned to transition to a Partial Hospitalization Program (PHP) for continued care [T264053-T264058].

sections.title: Psychiatric
sections.content: The patient has been diagnosed with major depressive disorder (recurrent, severe, without psychosis), generalized anxiety disorder (GAD), cannabis use disorder (severe), and chronic posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) [T264057, T264058]. The patient reported significant challenges including depression, anxiety, impulsivity issues such as gambling addiction and reckless driving behaviors. He also disclosed self-harm behaviors and suicidal ideation without intent [T264053]. Throughout treatment at the residential treatment center (RTC), the patient actively participated in group therapy sessions and worked on coping mechanisms for managing anxiety and relapse prevention strategies [T264055, T264056]. Medications were adjusted during his stay to address mood disturbances and sleep issues effectively. Upon discharge from RTC to a partial hospitalization program (PHP), the patient's mental status showed improvement with good sleep patterns, appetite normalization, restored energy levels, concentration back to baseline, no imminent danger to self or others observed [T264058].

sections.title: Medical
sections.content:
The patient has been diagnosed with Major Depressive Disorder (recurrent, severe, without psychosis), Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD), Cannabis Use Disorder (severe), and Posttraumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD) [T264053, T264054, T264055, T264057, T264058].  

Current psychiatric medications prescribed include:  
- Lamictal 200 mg p.o. q.a.m. for mood [T264053-T264058].  
- Lexapro 20 mg p.o. q.a.m. for depression [T264053-T264058].  
- Rexulti 2 mg p.o. q.a.m., initially prescribed but later discontinued after the addition of Seroquel [T264053-T264054].  
- Seroquel started at 50 mg p.o. at bedtime and titrated to 300 mg p.o. nightly by discharge to help augment mood and manage sleep issues [T264054-T264058].  
- Vistaril initially prescribed as 50 mg p.o. q6h prn for anxiety; increased to 75 mg during treatment but continued on a prn basis by discharge [T264053-T265057].   
- Trazodone was also available on a prn basis for sleep disturbances throughout treatment [T265053].

No current medical medications were reported or prescribed during the course of treatment [T265053-T265058].

The patient actively participated in group and individual therapy sessions focusing on coping mechanisms, relapse prevention strategies, family dynamics improvement through therapy sessions with his wife, and addressing substance abuse issues through AA meetings and education about abstinence from cannabis use[T265055,T265056,T265057].

By discharge from the residential treatment center program (RTC), the patient demonstrated improved energy levels and concentration back to baseline while reporting good sleep patterns without interruptions or cravings for cannabis[T265058].

sections.title: Psychosocial And Contextual Factors
sections.content:
The patient has a history of depression, anxiety, impulsive behaviors, and poor decision-making. He developed a gambling addiction over the past three months, resulting in significant financial losses and social isolation. The patient also reports engaging in reckless driving for 2.5 years due to anxiety and disclosed infidelity with his wife two weeks prior to admission. These issues have led to substantial life changes, including moving out of his marital home in November 2024 to live with his parents for support after experiencing a mental breakdown. The patient has been married for three years without children and currently works at a bank's fraud department call center but did not complete college education in computer science.

The patient's family psychiatric history includes parental struggles with alcoholism, depression, and drug addiction. He resides with his parents and younger brother while working on improving communication within the family through therapy sessions during treatment.

Additionally, the patient has been self-medicating daily with cannabis since age 16 (last use was on the day of admission) but denies cravings or use of other substances or alcohol abuse. He is actively participating in group therapy sessions as part of treatment at an adult residential treatment center (RTC). Despite challenges such as poor sleep patterns attributed to stress/anxiety/gambling behavior earlier on during care progression improved towards discharge readiness [T264053][T264054][T264055][T264056][T264057][T264058].

sections.title: Identifying Information
sections.content:
The patient was seen in the office for admission to the RTC level of care due to depression and anxiety. The patient reported feeling overwhelmed and described his life as "spiraling out of control" due to impulsive behaviors, including gambling addiction, reckless driving, and infidelity. He has a history of suicidal ideation without intent, self-harm behaviors, poor sleep (2-6 hours per night), and anxiety-related symptoms. The patient denies any history of physical, sexual, or emotional abuse or previous suicide attempts. He is married with no children and currently resides with his parents after moving out from his wife's home in November 2024 for mental health support. The patient works at a bank's fraud department call center and has been employed there for 1.5 years.

Psychiatric diagnoses include Major Depressive Disorder (recurrent-severe-without psychosis), Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD), Cannabis Use Disorder (severe), and Posttraumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD). Medical diagnosis includes none reported.

Current psychiatric medications prescribed are Lamictal 200 mg p.o q.a.m., Lexapro 20 mg p.o q.a.m., Seroquel up to 300 mg p.o nightly as adjusted during treatment progression, Vistaril on a p.r.n basis for anxiety increased up to 75 mg p.o q.6h during treatment adjustments; Trazodone on a p.r.n basis for sleep was also included initially but not mentioned later in discharge notes [T264053-T264058].

sections.title: Brief Summary Of Reasons For Admission To The Program
sections.content: The patient was admitted to the RTC level of care due to severe depression and anxiety. The patient reported feeling overwhelmed, with his life spiraling out of control, attributed to impulsive behaviors such as gambling addiction, reckless driving, and infidelity. He disclosed significant mental health symptoms leading to life changes including moving out of his marital home for support from his parents after a mental breakdown in November 2024. Additionally, the patient endorsed suicidal ideation without intent and self-harm behaviors. He has been self-medicating with cannabis daily since age 16 and reported poor sleep due to anxiety and stress. The psychiatric diagnoses include Major Depressive Disorder (recurrent-severe-without psychosis), Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD), Cannabis Use Disorder (severe), and later Posttraumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD) chronic [T264053][T264054][T264055][T264056].

sections.title: Summary Of Significant Findings On Initial Evaluation
sections.content:
The patient presented with significant mental health concerns, including feelings of being overwhelmed and a sense that his life was "spiraling out of control" due to impulsive behaviors and poor decision-making. Notable findings include:

1. **Psychiatric Symptoms**: The patient reported depression, anxiety, and a history of suicidal ideation over the past two weeks without intent but associated with guilt about past behaviors. He also disclosed self-harm behaviors such as scratching his skin and punching himself on the legs, with the last occurrence one week prior to evaluation.

2. **Behavioral Concerns**: The patient developed a gambling addiction over three months, spending $200-$1,000 daily at casinos resulting in a $15,000 loss. He also engaged in reckless driving 2-3 times per week for 2.5 years due to anxiety and admitted infidelity two weeks prior.

3. **Sleep Issues**: Reported sleeping only 2-6 hours per night without interruptions attributed to anxiety, stress, and gambling.

4. **Substance Use History**: Daily cannabis use since age 16; last use was on the morning of evaluation (02/11/2025). Denied cravings or use of alcohol or other illicit substances.

5. **Family Psychiatric History**: Both parents have histories of depression; mother has alcoholism while father has alcoholism and drug addiction.

6. **Mental Status Examination Findings**:
   - Appearance: Fair hygiene; casual dress.
   - Behavior: Calm and cooperative.
   - Mood/Affect: Depressed mood with congruent affect.
   - Thought Process/Content: Circumstantial thought process; denied active suicidal or homicidal ideation as well as hallucinations or paranoia.
   - Insight/Judgment: Limited insight and judgment were noted.

7. **Psychiatric Diagnoses at Admission**:
   - Major Depressive Disorder (recurrent-severe-without psychosis).
   - Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD).
   - Cannabis Use Disorder (severe).

8. **Medical Diagnoses at Admission**:
    None reported [Track T264053].

sections.title: Summary Of Important Medical Or Surgical Events During The Treatment Course
sections.content:
**SUMMARY OF IMPORTANT MEDICAL OR SURGICAL EVENTS DURING THE TREATMENT COURSE:**

The patient was admitted to the Residential Treatment Center (RTC) level of care due to severe mental health concerns, including Major Depressive Disorder (recurrent, severe, without psychosis), Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD), Cannabis Use Disorder (severe), and later diagnosed with Posttraumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD). The treatment course included individual and group psychotherapy sessions focusing on mood stabilization, anxiety management, coping mechanisms, relapse prevention strategies for cannabis use disorder, and addressing psychosocial issues. 

Throughout the treatment:
- The patient's psychiatric medications were adjusted multiple times to address ongoing symptoms such as poor sleep and anxiety. Seroquel dosage was gradually increased from 50 mg p.o. at bedtime [T264054] to 300 mg p.o. at bedtime by discharge [T264058]. Vistaril dosage was also increased from 50 mg p.o. q.6h p.r.n. [T264053] to 75 mg p.o. q.6h p.r.n., but its effectiveness remained under evaluation [T264056].
- The patient actively participated in therapy sessions and group activities while working on improving coping skills.
- Family therapy sessions were introduced but caused significant anxiety due to unresolved personal conflicts with his wife.
- By discharge, the patient reported improved sleep patterns and a return of energy levels and concentration back to baseline.

The patient completed RTC care successfully without any acute distress or imminent danger concerns at discharge and transitioned into a Partial Hospitalization Program (PHP) for continued support post-discharge [T264058].

sections.title: Course Of Treatment, Services Rendered, And The Progress Made By The Patient During Stay
sections.content:
The patient was admitted to the Adult Residential Treatment Center (RTC) level of care due to severe recurrent major depressive disorder without psychosis, generalized anxiety disorder (GAD), cannabis use disorder (severe), and chronic posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) [T264053; T264057; T264058]. The treatment plan included medication management, individual and group psychotherapy sessions, family therapy interventions, coping mechanism development, relapse prevention strategies for substance abuse recovery, and addressing psychosocial issues [T264053; T264054; T264055].

During the stay:
1. **Medication Adjustments**: 
   - Initial medications included Lamictal 200 mg p.o. q.a.m., Lexapro 20 mg p.o. q.a.m., Rexulti 2 mg p.o. q.a.m., Vistaril 50 mg p.o. q.6h p.r.n., and trazodone 50 mg at bedtime as needed [T264053].
   - Seroquel was introduced at a dose of 50 mg at bedtime on February 12th [T264054], increased progressively to target mood stabilization: first to 100 mg on February 19th [T264055], then to 200 mg on February 28th with plans for further increase if necessary [T264056], and finally adjusted to a stable dose of Seroquel at 300 mg nightly by March during discharge planning [T264057; T264058].
   - Vistaril dosage was increased from initial prescription of Vistaril 50mg p.r.n. to Vistaril increased dosage of up to75mg every six hours as needed for anxiety management by late February[T265056].

2. **Therapeutic Engagement**:
   - The patient actively participated in group therapy sessions focusing on coping skills development and relapse prevention strategies while working with therapists on future goals.
   - Family therapy sessions were planned but caused significant anxiety due to ongoing conflicts with his wife regarding personal issues discussed during treatment[T265055-T26557]

3 .Progression toward Discharge readiness observed improvement baseline energy concentration appetite sleep patterns normalized back baseline levels[T26558]

sections.title: Psychiatric
sections.content:
The patient has been diagnosed with Disruptive Mood Dysregulation Disorder (DMDD) and Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD), along with symptoms consistent with Cluster B pathology/limbic system instability. The patient has a history of significant psychosocial stressors, including early childhood sexual abuse, recent sexual trauma, emotional abuse by adoptive parents, and a challenging family dynamic. The patient also reports ongoing emotional instability characterized by mood swings, irritability, poor distress tolerance, intrusive thoughts related to past trauma, and intermittent passive self-harm thoughts without intent or plan. 

The treatment plan includes residential care for safety and stabilization while addressing mood disturbances through medication management and therapy focused on coping skills development, emotional regulation strategies such as DBT (Dialectical Behavior Therapy), anger management techniques, self-esteem building exercises, family dynamics improvement efforts as well as academic support. Current psychiatric medications include Lexapro 20mg QAM for depression/anxiety [T264080-T264084], Lamictal 200mg QAM with titration to 150mg QHS [T264083] increasing to 200mg BID [T264084], and Hydroxyzine 50mg BID PRN for breakthrough worry/acute agitation/sleep maintenance [T264079-T264084]. 

The patient's prognosis is described as fair to guarded depending on participation in the therapeutic process. sections.title: Summary Of Significant Findings On Initial Evaluation
sections.content:
The patient presented for admission to the RTC level of care due to mood swings, impulsivity, and recent withdrawal from school. She reported a long history of behavioral issues starting at age 12 and expressed feelings of hopelessness and emotional pain. The patient disclosed a history of sexual abuse at age 3 in an in-home daycare setting, with the abuser reportedly committing suicide. Additionally, she reported a recent incident on 02/17/2025 involving sexual activity under the influence of marijuana and alcohol; however, her father stated it was consensual. The patient also endorsed lifelong emotional abuse by her adoptive parents but denied physical abuse or hallucinations.

She has no history of suicide attempts but admitted to self-harm (cutting) over the past two years with no medical attention required for injuries. Her sleep pattern is erratic (1-14 hours per night), appetite decreased following recent stressors, and she denies experiencing nightmares or dreaming.

The patient's psychiatric diagnosis includes Disruptive Mood Dysregulation Disorder (DMDD) and Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD). She has been on Lexapro 10mg daily for one month prior to admission. Past psychiatric interventions include voluntary inpatient rehab in 2019 for three months and placement in a therapeutic group home from 2020-2023 due to behavioral concerns.

Her mental status examination revealed good hygiene, calm behavior with appropriate eye contact, normal speech patterns without hallucinations or paranoia but limited insight/judgment. Her mood was described as depressed with congruent affect.

Treatment recommendations included admission to RTC care for stabilization through therapy targeting coping skills development alongside medication management [T264072].

sections.title: Summary Of Important Medical Or Surgical Events During The Treatment Course
sections.content:
The patient was admitted to residential treatment center (RTC) level of care due to mood swings, impulsivity, and emotional instability associated with Disruptive Mood Dysregulation Disorder (DMDD) and Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD). Throughout the treatment course, the patient underwent multiple assessments and medication adjustments aimed at stabilizing mood, improving distress tolerance, and addressing symptoms of anxiety and depression.

1. **Initial Admission**: The patient was placed on Lexapro 10mg QAM for depression management [T264072]. 

2. **Medication Adjustments**:
   - Lexapro dosage was progressively increased from 10mg QAM to 20mg QAM over several weeks for enhanced efficacy in managing depressive symptoms [T264073-T264084].
   - Lamictal was introduced at an initial dose of 25mg QAM with titration planned up to therapeutic levels. It reached a dose of 200mg QAM with additional evening doses added for mood stability [T264073-T264084].
   - Hydroxyzine PRN usage began at a dose of 50mg BID targeting acute agitation/worry/sleep maintenance during Lamictal titration [T264075-T264084].

3. **Psychiatric Symptoms Progression**:
   - The patient experienced persistent irritability, emotional lability, intrusive thoughts related to past trauma, sleep disturbances including overnight awakenings affecting energy levels.
   - New onset auditory hallucinations were reported but later resolved as "echoes in my head" following medication adjustments [T264077-T264078].
   - Despite ongoing psychosocial stressors such as interpersonal distancing from parents and uncertainty about living arrangements post-treatment discharge, relative improvement in anxiety symptoms and emotional regulation skills were noted towards the later stages of treatment [T264081-T264084].

4. **Medical Event**: The patient developed upper respiratory infection treated empirically with Amoxicillin 875mg BID x7 days; laboratory evaluations ruled out viral infection [T264077].

5. **Therapeutic Engagement**:
   - Participation in DBT groups/activities focused on coping skills development.
   - Psychoeducation sessions targeted emotional regulation strategies like STOP skill implementation.

6. **Treatment Plan Evolution**:
    Continued focus on stabilization through RTC level care included observation for safety concerns while addressing underlying psychosocial factors contributing to distress tolerance issues.

No surgical events were documented during this period.

References: T264072 through T284084

sections.title: Course Of Treatment, Services Rendered, And The Progress Made By The Patient During Stay
sections.content:
The patient was admitted to residential treatment care (RTC) for stabilization of mood and management of Disruptive Mood Dysregulation Disorder (DMDD) and Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD). The treatment plan included medication adjustments, therapy sessions targeting emotional regulation skills, coping mechanisms, trauma processing, and family dynamics improvement. 

Initially prescribed Lexapro 10mg QAM for depression [T264072], the dosage was progressively increased to 15mg QAM [T264073] and later to 20mg QAM [T264079]. Lamictal was introduced at 25mg QAM with a titration schedule up to therapeutic dosing of 200mg QAM and additional evening doses reaching up to 150mg QHS [T264084]. Hydroxyzine PRN at a dose of 50mg BID was added for breakthrough worry/acute agitation/sleep maintenance [T264075].

Throughout her stay:
- The patient reported marginal improvements in mood stability but continued experiencing irritability, emotional lability, intrusive thoughts related to past trauma, sleep disturbances due to environmental factors in the milieu setting, diminished energy levels on certain days following poor sleep quality or heightened distress episodes.
- She endorsed ongoing psychosocial stressors such as interpersonal distancing from parents and uncertainty regarding living arrangements post-treatment.
- Despite limited motivation towards therapeutic engagement initially noted during assessments [e.g., T264082], she demonstrated gradual improvement in utilizing emotional regulation skills like deep breathing exercises. She expressed preference for continuing current medication regimens while advocating further titration schedules targeting maximum benefit.

The patient's progress included relative improvements in anxiety symptoms ("my anxiety is not so strong" - T264081), better identification of emotions ("I can catch it quicker" - T264081), reduced utilization of PRN medications indicating improved distress tolerance (reported during assessment on May 13th - T264084), alongside consistent participation in DBT groups.

Overall prognosis remained guarded with plans emphasizing continued observation under RTC level care focusing on safety stabilization measures while addressing underlying psychosocial contributing factors through individualized therapeutic interventions. Estimated length of stay ranged between six weeks up until clinically indicated discharge based upon symptomatic progression observed across follow-up assessments spanning February-May 2025.

sections.title: Recommendations And Arrangements For Future Treatment
sections.content:
The patient is recommended to continue residential level of care for observation of safety, symptomatic stabilization, maintenance of functioning, and prevention of further decline in functioning. The treatment plan includes engagement in an individualized therapeutic course targeting underlying psychosocial contributing factors, emotional regulation skills, coping skills, symptomatic management, and interpersonal family dynamic/communication. Medication adjustments are planned based on symptom progression and tolerance.

Specific recommendations include:
1. **Medication Management**: Continuation and titration of psychiatric medications such as Lexapro 20mg QAM for mood and anxiety; Lamictal with increased dosing to 200mg QAM and 150mg QHS (pending symptom progression); Hydroxyzine 50mg BID PRN for breakthrough worry/acute agitation/sleep maintenance [T264084].
2. **Therapeutic Engagement**: Focus on emotional regulation skills development through DBT groups or activities.
3. **Psychosocial Support**: Address ongoing stressors related to social disposition (e.g., housing arrangements) while improving distress tolerance.
4. **Sleep Hygiene Counseling**: Recommendations provided regarding sleep habits to address intermittent overnight awakenings [T264084].

She reports her depression progressed to the point where she was "just lying in bed a lot", not really doing activities, and beginning to have suicidal thoughts. She reports feeling empty and sad almost every day, no longer having interest in previously enjoyed activities, having low energy, poor concentration, and passive suicidal thoughts (she denies plan or intent to end her life). She reports having high anxiety, frequently feeling over worried, tense, on edge, and having a hard time controlling her worry. She is currently on Prozac, Abilify, and lithium. She denies symptoms suggestive for an episode of mania or hypomania, she reports the lithium was started to help with her suicidal thoughts. She reports her first night here, last night, trazodone did not help with sleep and she is requesting a higher dose. She denies current medication side effect.

sections.title: Identifying Information
sections.content:
The patient is an African American female who has been readmitted to the RTC level of care following a Baker Act hospitalization for suicidal ideation and psychosis. She has a history of schizoaffective disorder, bipolar type, and generalized anxiety disorder. The patient reports experiencing disorganized thoughts, auditory hallucinations (choir songs stuck in her head), and poor sleep (3-4 hours per night). She denies visual hallucinations or command hallucinations. Her psychiatric history includes multiple Baker Acts since 2013 for suicidal ideation and psychosis, with the most recent occurring from 02/05/2025 to 02/11/2025. The patient also has a history of depression starting at age 16, self-harm beginning in third grade, numerous suicide attempts (e.g., hanging attempt at age 15; consuming Risperdal in 2021; drinking bleach in 2022), sexual abuse reported on 01/10/2025, and legal issues including arrests for felony mischief (fire incident) in 2021 and probation violation in 2023.

She is homeless since approximately one year ago but lives with her mother, father (parents divorced during elementary school), brother aged 27, sister aged 18. She holds an associate's degree in English literature but is currently unemployed. Her daughter was born when she was aged nineteen; parental rights were terminated—daughter now resides with biological relatives.

sections.title: Brief Summary Of Reasons For Admission To The Program
sections.content:
The patient was admitted to the RTC level of care following a Baker Act due to suicidal ideation and psychosis. The patient has a history of schizoaffective disorder, bipolar type, and generalized anxiety disorder. She presented with disorganized thoughts, tangential thinking, poor insight and judgment, auditory hallucinations (choir songs), and a history of multiple suicide attempts. The patient also reported experiencing intense emotions beyond sadness not related to anger, irritability, flight of ideas, decreased need for sleep, anxiety symptoms such as excessive worry and muscle tension, restlessness, poor sleep patterns (3-4 hours per night), guilt over setting boundaries with others, and feelings of being perceived negatively by others upon returning to treatment [T264059].  

Additionally noted were her struggles with homelessness since 2023; past arrests in 2021 for felony mischief (starting a fire) and in 2023 for probation violation; self-reported sexual abuse; family psychiatric history including maternal stress-induced seizures and paternal alcoholism; as well as her goal for "complete mental health reformation" [T264059].  

The justification for admission includes the intensity of services required due to ongoing mood disturbances (dysphoria/anxiety), thought disorganization (tangential/loose associations), passive suicidal ideation without active plans or intent at times during treatment progression [T264060-T264065], guarded behavior initially improving gradually through structured therapeutic interventions [T264063-T264065], continued challenges in social functioning despite gradual improvement in mood stability/thought organization under medication management [T264064-T264065], alongside disposition planning needs given her homeless status impacting compliance potential with alternative outpatient treatments like Clozaril consideration earlier rejected based on logistical barriers cited therein[T264061].

sections.title: Summary Of Significant Findings On Initial Evaluation
sections.content:
The patient was re-admitted to the RTC level of care following a Baker Act hospitalization due to suicidal ideation and psychosis. The initial evaluation revealed disorganized and tangential thoughts, poor insight and judgment, auditory hallucinations (choir songs stuck in her head), interrupted sleep (3-4 hours per night), fair appetite, and a history of depression starting at age 16. The patient reported multiple suicide attempts over the years, including hanging herself with a scarf at age 15, consuming Risperdal in 2021, and drinking bleach in 2022. She also endorsed self-harm behaviors beginning in third grade but denied recent incidents or medical attention for cuts.

The patient has been diagnosed with schizoaffective disorder-bipolar type and generalized anxiety disorder. She presents with symptoms such as irritability, flight of ideas, decreased need for sleep, anxiety, excessive worry, muscle tension, restlessness, poor sleep quality due to environmental factors during hospitalization ("it was really loud"), dysphoric mood described as depressed/anxious/guarded/dysphoric across evaluations.

Her mental status examination showed guarded behavior; pressured speech; tangential thought process; flat affect; low energy/concentration levels; passive suicidal ideation without active plans or intent; no visual hallucinations/delusions/SI/HI actively reported [T264059][T264060][T264061][T264062][T264063].

sections.title: Summary Of Important Medical Or Surgical Events During The Treatment Course
sections.content:
The patient was re-admitted to the RTC level of care following a Baker Act from hospital RTC due to suicidal ideation and psychosis. The treatment course included multiple evaluations and adjustments in psychiatric medications, as well as participation in individual and group therapy sessions. The patient presented with symptoms of schizoaffective disorder, bipolar type, generalized anxiety disorder, disorganized thoughts, tangential speech, poor insight and judgment, dysphoric mood, anxiety, auditory hallucinations (choir songs), and a history of suicidal ideation.

Key events during the treatment course include:
1. Initial admission on 02/11/2025 for stabilization after experiencing suicidal ideation with a plan involving poisoning herself.
2. Gradual medication adjustments were made throughout the treatment period:
   - Abilify dosage increased progressively from 7.5 mg p.o. nightly [T264059] to 30 mg p.o. nightly [T264063].
   - Lithium maintained at 300 mg p.o., initially b.i.d., later adjusted to t.i.d [T264063].
   - Lamictal dosage increased from 50 mg p.o. b.i.d [T264059] to 100 mg p.o. b.i.d [T264065].
   - Remeron added at 15 mg p.o. nightly starting from T264061.
   - Pristiq introduced at an initial dose of 25 mg q.a.m., later increased to 50 mg q.a.m by T264064.
3. Patient demonstrated gradual improvement in mood stability but continued struggles with thought organization and tangential thinking.
4. Participation in therapeutic activities varied; while attending groups was reported inconsistently by staff observations (quiet or aloof), one-on-one interactions showed less disorganization over time.

No surgical events were noted during this period.

Citations: [T264059], [T264060], [T264061], [T264062], [T264063], [T264064], [T264065].

sections.title: Course Of Treatment, Services Rendered, And The Progress Made By The Patient During Stay
sections.content:
The patient was admitted to the Adult RTC program for stabilization of mood and psychotic disturbances following a history of schizoaffective disorder, bipolar type, and generalized anxiety disorder. The treatment plan included medication management, individual and group psychotherapy sessions, reality testing, coping skills development, anger and anxiety management strategies, self-esteem improvement efforts, self-regulation techniques as well as disposition planning [T264059][T264060][T264061][T264062][T264063][T264064].

During the stay:
1. **Medication Adjustments**: The patient's psychiatric medications were reviewed multiple times with adjustments made to dosages for optimal therapeutic effect. Medications included Abilify (increased from 7.5 mg p.o. nightly to 30 mg p.o. nightly), Lithium (300 mg p.o., increased from b.i.d. to t.i.d.), Lamictal (increased from 50 mg p.o. b.i.d to 100 mg p.o b.i.d.), Remeron (15 mg p.o nightly added), Pristiq (added at 25-50 mg q.a.m.), Vistaril and trazodone on a prn basis [T264059][T264060][T264061][T264062][T264063].

2. **Therapeutic Interventions**: Structured therapeutic environments were provided alongside individual therapy sessions focusing on reality testing and supportive psychotherapy aimed at improving thought processes and adaptive functioning [T264060-T265065]. Group therapy participation was encouraged; however, the patient displayed varying levels of engagement due to her guarded behavior [T265065].

3. **Mental Status Monitoring**: Regular mental status examinations revealed gradual improvements in mood stability with less intrusive thoughts over time but persistent tangential thinking patterns requiring redirection during interactions [Track Numbers T265061-T265065]. Insight and judgment improved slightly but remained fair overall.

4. **Progress Observed**: Over time during her stay:
   - The patient reported feeling less anxious.
   - Sleep quality improved along with appetite.
   - Energy levels remained low but concentration showed slight improvement.
   - Thoughts became more organized though still tangential at times.
   - Participation in groups increased gradually despite initial withdrawal tendencies [Track Numbers T265063-T265065].
   
5. **Disposition Planning**: Efforts were made towards disposition planning considering the patient's homelessness status while exploring future goals for reintegration into society post-treatment [Track Numbers T265064-T265065].

Overall progress was noted as gradual improvement in mood stability with continued challenges related to disorganized thought processes requiring structured support systems throughout her estimated length of stay of approximately three weeks or as clinically indicated based on ongoing assessments[T26059].
